3: idou he skene tou theou meta ton anthropon kai skemnosei met auton: 'Behold the dwelling place (skene) of God is with people, and he will dwell (skenosei) with them.' The skene-language deliberately echoes the wilderness tabernacle (Mishkan) and anticipates John 1:14 where the Word eskenosen (tabernacled) among us. The eschatological new creation is the realization of what the tabernacle mediated: direct divine presence without the veil, without the intermediary system, without separation — YHWH's face-to-face dwelling with his people. The Sinai covenant promise reaches its telos in the new Jerusalem.
1:
kainos ouranos kai kainē gē — kainos (qualitatively new, not chronologically new; contrast neos) is the word Revelation uses throughout the new-creation vision. The distinction matters: this is not a renovated cosmos but a new one of a different order altogether. The same word appears in 2 Corinthians 5:17 (new creation) and Isaiah 65:17 LXX.

ho gar prōtos ouranos kai hē prōtē gē apēlthan: the first heaven and earth apēlthan (went away, departed) — the same verb used in 21:4 for the old order passing. The absence of the sea (hē thalassa ouk estin eti) is significant: in ANE cosmology and in OT imagery, the sea represents primordial chaos (Genesis 1:2), the domain of the beast (Revelation 13:1), and the barrier that separates. Its disappearance signals the end of chaos, threat, and separation.
2:
tēn polin tēn hagian Ierousalēm kainēn eidon katabainousan ek tou ouranou apo tou theou: the city is katabainousan (descending, present participle) — an ongoing, visionary descent. The new Jerusalem is not a human achievement ascending from below but a divine gift descending from above. The grammar signals its character: this city originates with God, not with human civilization or religion.

hētoimasmenēn hōs nymphen kekosmēmenēn tō andri autēs: prepared as a bride adorned for her husband. The bride metaphor activates the OT covenant-marriage imagery (Isaiah 61:10; Revelation 62:5; Jeremiah 2:2; Ezek 16) where YHWH is husband and Israel is bride. The new Jerusalem as the bride of the Lamb (Revelation 21:9) is the eschatological fulfillment of the entire covenant relationship: the People of God perfected and united with the God who covenanted with them.
4:
kai exaleipsei pan dakryon ek tōn ophthalmōn autōn: exaleipsei (will wipe out, erase completely; future active) — the same root is used in Acts 3:19 for the erasure of sins. God erases tears as decisively as he forgives guilt: both are obliterated, not merely set aside. The promise quotes Isaiah 25:8 (YHWH will swallow up death and wipe away tears from all faces) and reverses the mourning of Isaiah 65:19.

ho thanatos ouk estai eti — ouk ... eti (no longer, not any more): categorical negation with emphasis. Death itself ceases; this is not survival of death but its abolition. Ta prōta apēlthan (the first things have gone away) uses the same apēlthan as Revelation 21:1 — the old order departs as a unified whole: death, grief, pain, the sea, the first heaven and earth all belong to the old order that is now gone.
5:
idou kaina poiō panta: kaina (qualitatively new) with the present tense poiō (I am making) — the new creation is announced as a present act of the divine will, not a future plan. The one speaking is seated on the throne: in Revelation's theology the throne is shared between God the Father and the Lamb (Revelation 22:1, 3), so the speaker carries the full divine authority that opened the cosmos in Genesis.

hoti houtoi hoi logoi pistoi kai alēthinoi eisin: the authentication formula (repeated at Revelation 22:6; Revelation 19:9) — these words are pistoi kai alēthinoi (faithful and true), the divine self-description from Revelation 3:14 and Revelation 19:11. The words are vouched for by their source: the one who is Faithful-and-True declares that these words share his own character.
6:
gegonen: the perfect active of ginomai — "It has happened and stands accomplished." The same verb Jesus uses on the cross (tetelestai in John, but here using ginomai) signals the consummation of redemptive history. The use of the perfect (not the aorist) is precise: the new creation is not just completed but completed with permanent effect.

ego eimi to Alpha kai to Omega, hē archē kai to telos: the Alpha-Omega formula here is attributed to God the Father (as in Revelation 1:8); in 22:13 the same three-part formula (Alpha/Omega + First/Last + Beginning/End) is applied to Christ — the shared title making the Christological identification explicit. Hē archē kai to telos (the Beginning and the End) is new in Revelation 21:6, expanding the pair from Revelation 1; all three formulas converge in Revelation 22:13.

tō dipsōnti dōsō ek tēs pēgēs tou hydatos tēs zōēs dōrean: dōrean (freely, as gift, at no cost) — this adverb characterizes the gift as grace: the water of life is not earned or purchased. The thirst imagery activates Isaiah 55:1 (come to the waters, you who are thirsty; come and buy without money) and John 4:14 (the water I give will become a spring welling up to eternal life).
7:
ho nikōn klēronomēsei tauta: ho nikōn (the one who conquers, present participle — continuous overcoming) echoes the promise to the nikōn in each of the seven letters (Revelation 2–3). Klēronomēsei (will inherit) — inheritance language recalls the covenant promise to Abraham (Gen 12; 15; 17) and the promised land; here it is eschatological: the whole new creation is the inheritance of those who endure.

kai esomai autō theos kai autos estai moi [ho] huios: the covenant formula applied universally. 2 Samuel 7:14 speaks of the Davidic king ("I will be his father and he will be my son"); Psalms 89:26-27 applies it to the one who cries "My Father." Revelation applies this father-son adoption language to every overcomer — the promises made to the messianic king are extended to all who are in Christ.
8:
hoi deiloi: the cowardly/faithless-under-pressure — placed first in the exclusion list, ahead of murderers and sorcerers. Deilos is used in Mark 4:40 when Jesus rebukes the disciples' fear in the storm ("Why are you so afraid? Have you no faith?"). In Revelation's context of Roman persecution, the cowardly are those who apostatize to avoid suffering — a more acute pastoral concern than abstract vices.

ho thanatos ho deuteros: the second death. Revelation defines it as the lake of fire (Revelation 20:14). The "second death" is a concept found in Targum literature (Tg. Deuteronomy 33:6; Tg. Isaiah 22:14; Revelation 65:6) where it means permanent, irreversible exclusion from God. Revelation adopts and crystalizes the term. The first death (biological) may claim the faithful; the second death (eternal exclusion) cannot (Revelation 2:11; Revelation 20:6).
9: deuro, deixō soi tēn nymphen tēn gynaika tou arniou: "Come, I will show you the bride, the wife of the Lamb." This invitation deliberately mirrors 17:1 where the same angel invites John: "Come, I will show you the judgment of the great prostitute." The two visions are paired: Babylon the harlot (Revelation 17–18) vs. Jerusalem the bride (Revelation 21). The angel offers the same transportation to see radically different destinations — judgment and redemption shown by the same guide.
10: kai apēnegken me en pneumati epi oros mega kai hypsēlon: transported en pneumati (in the Spirit) to a great and high mountain. This visionary transportation echoes Ezekiel 40:1-2 precisely: the Spirit brings Ezekiel to "a very high mountain" to see the new temple city. John's new Jerusalem vision is the fulfillment of Ezekiel's vision — the mountain-top perspective enabling the Seer to receive what Ezekiel received as promise. The Spirit-transportation also echoes Ezekiel 3:14; Revelation 8:3; Revelation 11:1, 24.
11:
echousan tēn doxan tou theou: "having the glory of God" — the Shekinah, the divine radiance that filled the tabernacle (Exodus 40:34-35) and the Solomonic temple (1 Kings 8:10-11), and that Ezekiel saw depart from the temple at the Babylonian conquest (Ezekiel 10:18-19; Revelation 11:22-23), now permanently dwelling in the new Jerusalem. The city does not merely reflect glory — it has it as its intrinsic characteristic.

hō phōstēr autēs homoios lithō timiōtatō hōs lithō iaspidi krystalloeidei: the city's radiance compared to jasper clear as crystal. Jasper (iaspis) is used both for the appearance of the one on the throne (Revelation 4:3) and now for the city that houses his presence — the city's appearance mirrors the divine glory itself. The precious stones throughout Revelation 21:19-21 echo Ezekiel 28:13 (the king of Tyre adorned with precious stones in Eden) and the high-priestly breastplate (Exodus 28:17-20).
12: echousa teicho mega kai hypsēlon echousa pylōnas dōdeka kai epi tois pylōsin angelous dōdeka kai onomata epigegrammena ha estin [ta onomata] tōn dōdeka phylōn huiōn Israēl: the wall with twelve gates and twelve angels, the gates bearing the names of the twelve tribes. Ezekiel 48:31-35 already assigned the twelve gates of the ideal city to the twelve tribes. The angels at the gates function as guardians — but also as witnesses to the access structure: the new Jerusalem is entered through the covenant people (the tribes) who represent the full scope of Israel's election.
13: apo anatolēs pylōnes treis kai apo borra pylōnes treis kai apo notou pylōnes treis kai apo dysmōn pylōnes treis: three gates on each of the four sides, facing all compass points. This orientation mirrors both Ezekiel 48:31-34 (same arrangement for the ideal city's gates) and the arrangement of the tribes around the tabernacle in the wilderness (Num 2). The four-directional opening signals the universal accessibility of the new Jerusalem: approach from every direction is possible. No gate is the "back entrance."
14: kai to teicho tēs poleōs echōn thremeliou dōdeka kai ep autōn dōdeka onomata tōn dōdeka apostolōn tou arniou: twelve foundations inscribed with the names of the twelve apostles. Gates named for the tribes (OT); foundations named for the apostles (NT). The city is built on both testimonies — the continuity of OT and NT revelation architecturally expressed. Ephesians 2:20 speaks of the church "built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets"; here that Pauline metaphor becomes literal architecture in the new Jerusalem. The apostles are apostles tou Arniou (of the Lamb) — their authority derives from and belongs to the Lamb.
15: ho lalōn met emou eichen metron kalamon chrysoun: a golden measuring rod. Measuring in apocalyptic literature signals divine claiming and protection (Ezek 40; Zechariah 2:1-5; Revelation 11:1-2). That the measuring rod is chrysoun (golden) matches the city's materials — the instrument of measurement is made of what the city is made of, signaling that the city fully meets the divine standard. The angel's measurement is an act of divine affirmation: this city is exactly what God intends.
16: kai hē polis tetragonos keitai kai to mēkos autēs hoson [kai] to platos: the city is tetragonos (four-cornered, square — or by extension, cubic) — equal in length, width, and height. 12,000 stadia in each dimension. The cubic shape recalls the Holy of Holies in Solomon's temple (1 Kings 6:20: twenty cubits in length, width, and height — a perfect cube). The entire new Jerusalem is the Holy of Holies — the inner sanctuary of divine presence expanded to cosmic proportions. What was restricted to the high priest once a year is now the entire dwelling of the redeemed.
17: kai emethrēsen to teicho autou hekatōn tessarakonta tessarōn pēchōn metrou anthrōpou ho estin angelou: 144 cubits (= 12 × 12) for the wall — another multiple of 12, the number of completeness in Revelation (twelve tribes, twelve apostles, 144,000). The curious note that the human measurement is also the angel's measurement (metrou anthrōpou ho estin angelou) may signal the unity of human and angelic existence in the new creation: the customary distinction between earthly and heavenly scales no longer applies.
18: kai hē endōmēsis tou teichous autēs iaspis kai hē polis chrysos katharos homoios hyalō katharō: endōmēsis is a rare word (possibly a hapax in biblical Greek) for "building material" or "construction." Pure gold transparent as glass is physically impossible — gold is opaque. The description signals that the new creation's materials exceed what the old physics permits: this is not renovated matter but transfigured matter, the stuff of a world where divine glory permeates everything. The transparency of gold implies there is nothing hidden, no opacity between creature and Creator.
19: hoi themelioi tou teichous tēs poleōs panti lithō timiō kekosmēmenoi: the twelve foundations adorned with precious stones. The list (jasper, sapphire, agate, emerald, onyx, ruby, chrysolite, beryl, topaz, chrysoprase, jacinth, amethyst) parallels but does not perfectly match the high priestly breastplate of Exodus 28:17-20 — eight of the twelve correspond. The LXX order differs from the MT. The connection to the breastplate is deliberate: the high priest bore the names of the twelve tribes over his heart as he entered the sanctuary; the new Jerusalem's foundations bear the apostles' names. The priestly imagery is transferred from one individual mediator to the entire apostolic foundation of the city.
20: ho pemptos sardonyx ho hektos sardion ho hebdomos chrysolithos ho ogdoos bēryllos ho ennatos topazion ho dekatos chrysoprason ho hendekatos hyakinthos ho dōdekatos amethystos: the list of the final eight foundation stones. Ancient gemstone terminology is imprecise and identification of the exact minerals is uncertain — Greek and Hebrew stone names do not map neatly onto modern mineralogical categories. What is clear is the visual effect: the twelve foundations are a spectrum of color, light, and brilliance. The cumulative image is not a building inventory but a vision of transcendent beauty — the city adorned as a bride (Revelation 21:2) made architectural.
21:
kai hoi dōdeka pylōnes dōdeka margaritai, heis hekastos tōn pylōnōn ēn ex henos margaritou: each gate a single pearl. A pearl of sufficient size to serve as a city gate is obviously beyond natural possibility — the image functions as a superlative of purity and singular beauty. Pearls in the ancient world were considered the most precious of gems (Matthew 13:46 uses the single great pearl as the image for the kingdom). Each gate is irreducibly one thing — unified, perfect, unbroken.

kai hē plateia tēs poleōs chrysos katharos hōs hyalos diaphanes: the main street of pure gold transparent as glass. Plateia (broad way, main street, public space) is singular — the whole city has one street, or the entire ground level is this open transparent space. The transparency motif recurs: nothing opaque, nothing hidden, divine light permeating the material structure of the city itself.
22:
kai naon ouk eidon en autē: "I did not see a temple in it." The deliberate absence. The temple was the locus of divine presence in the old order — the place where heaven and earth intersected, where priests mediated access to God, where sacrifice was offered. Its absence is not a loss but a fulfillment: there is no need for a mediated presence when the presence is direct and total.

ho gar Kyrios ho Theos ho Pantokrator naos autēs estin kai to Arnion: the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple. God and the Lamb together constitute the temple — singular — the co-divine presence that fills the new Jerusalem without boundary or mediation. This fulfills the trajectory from Exodus 25:8 (let them make me a sanctuary that I may dwell among them) through John 2:19-21 (the temple he spoke of was his body) to Revelation 21:3 (the dwelling of God is with people). The tabernacle, the temple, the incarnate Christ, and the new Jerusalem form one continuous line of divine-presence theology.
23:
kai hē polis ou chreian echei tou hēliou oude tēs selēnēs hina phainōsin autē: "the city has no need of the sun or moon to shine on it." This fulfills Isaiah 60:19-20 verbatim in sense: "No longer will you have the sun for light by day ... for the LORD will be your everlasting light." The sun and moon are part of the old creation's luminous order; in the new creation the divine glory replaces them.

hē gar doxa tou theou ephōtisen autēn kai ho lychnos autēs to Arnion: the glory (doxa, Shekinah) of God is the city's light source, and the Lamb is its lychnos (lamp, luminous body). The same Christ who declared "I am the light of the world" (John 8:12; Revelation 9:5) is the lamp of the new Jerusalem. The divine glory and the Lamb's lamp are distinguished but function together — the Trinitarian shape of the new creation's illumination.
24:
kai peripatēsousin ta ethnē dia tou phōtos autēs: "the nations will walk by its light." The inclusion of ta ethnē (the nations/Gentiles) as permanent inhabitants and participants fulfills the long prophetic vision of Isaiah 60:3 (nations shall come to your light) and Zechariah 14:16 (all nations will go up year after year to worship the King). This is not Gentile conversion to Israel's religion but the eschatological unity of all peoples in the new Jerusalem — the culmination of the Abrahamic promise that all nations would be blessed (Genesis 12:3).

kai hoi basileis tēs gēs pherousin tēn doxan autōn eis autēn: the kings of the earth bring their doxa (glory, splendor) into the city. These are the same basileis tēs gēs who throughout Revelation opposed God and the Lamb (Revelation 6:15; Revelation 17:2; Revelation 18:3). Their tribute in the new creation reverses their rebellion in the old order: those who resisted now bring honor freely. This is not universalism but the comprehensive sovereignty of the new creation encompassing even those who opposed it.
25:
kai hoi pylōnes autēs ou mē kleisthōsin hēmeras: "its gates shall never be shut by day" — the gates of the ancient city were shut at night for security. The new Jerusalem's gates never close because there is no threat, no night, no hostile force that could require them. Open gates signal total security: the city is not fortified against enemies because there are none. The promise fulfills Isaiah 60:11 (your gates shall be open continually, day and night they shall not be shut).

nyx gar ouk estai ekei: "for there will be no night there." Night in Revelation is associated with darkness, hiddenness, and the activities of evil (1 Thessalonians 5:5-7; John 3:19). Its abolition in the new Jerusalem (repeated in Revelation 22:5) is not merely a meteorological observation but a theological one: the conditions for evil, deception, and darkness no longer obtain.
26: kai oisousin tēn doxan kai tēn timēn tōn ethnōn eis autēn: "they will bring the glory and honor of the nations into it." Doxa kai timē (glory and honor) — the paired terms used of God's worthiness in doxological contexts (Revelation 4:9, 11; Revelation 5:12-13). That the nations bring doxa implies that what the nations had — their cultures, achievements, excellences — is not destroyed in the new creation but transformed and offered. The new Jerusalem receives the best of human history, transfigured and consecrated.
27:
kai ou mē eiselthē eis autēn pan koinon kai [ho] poiōn bdelygma kai pseudos: the double negative ou mē with the aorist subjunctive is emphatic prohibition — nothing common/impure shall enter. Koinon (common, defiling — the cultic term for what violates ritual purity; Acts 10:14-15 uses it for what Peter considered unclean). Bdelygma (abomination) echoes the "abomination of desolation" (Daniel 9:27; Revelation 11:31; Mark 13:14) — here permanently excluded rather than temporarily installed in the temple.

ei mē hoi gegrammenoi en tō bibliō tēs zōēs tou Arniou: "except those written in the Lamb's book of life." The only qualification for entry is name-inscription by the Lamb — not ethnic identity, moral achievement, or ritual status, but the Lamb's own record. The biblion tēs zōēs (book of life) appears throughout Revelation (Revelation 3:5; Revelation 13:8; Revelation 17:8; Revelation 20:12, 15) as the definitive eschatological register. It belongs to the Lamb: he holds the criterion of entry.
